items.title: Why are young men turning to steroids? | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Bigorexia, or muscle dysmorphia, is a growing concern among young men. No matter how much muscle they build, many never see themselves as big enough.  

As SARMs and anabolic steroids become easier to find online — often marketed like ordinary supplements and promoted across social media — they've become increasingly visible to young men. More are taking serious risks in pursuit of the "ideal" physique, and some have paid for it with their health. 

One statistic is particularly striking: steroid dependence rates are estimated to be higher than heroin, cocaine, or alcohol. 

Watch the full documentary "Muscle mania and the cult of masculinity - When the body becomes an obsession" on the DW Documentary YouTube channel.

items.author: Vox
items.content:
<article>For years, right-wing influencers and politicians have pushed the conservative movement to have more controversial, anti-establishment views. Why are some of those figures now upset about where the movement has ended up?

Subscribe to our channel! http://goo.gl/0bsAjO

In today’s episode, guest host Bryan Walsh speaks with Vox Senior Correspondent Zack Beauchamp about conservatives who decry the extremist direction that the right is moving but refuse to recognize the role they played in pushing the movement in that direction. The two discuss US Rep. Thomas Massie (R-KY), pundit Ben Shapiro, podcaster Candace Owens, the drivers of conservative extremism, and what the party might look like after Donald Trump.

items.title: Hitler's Death Army: The Complete Story Of The Das Reich SS Division
items.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
items.content:
<article>On June 6, 1944, the elite Second SS Panzer Division Das Reich was ordered to the Normandy front. As they marched across France, they left a horrific trail of massacres, including the complete destruction of Oradour-sur-Glane. Delayed by courageous French Resistance and SOE operatives like Violette Szabo, the division arrived too late to stop the Allied invasion and was ultimately decimated. Discover the dark history of Hitler's most brutal division and the controversial post-war trials that followed their escape from justice.

items.title: Great advice from an astronaut 🧑🏿‍🚀⭐🚀
items.author: PBS
items.content:
<article>Former astronaut Charles Bolden recalled an encounter in the late ’70s with Ron McNair, one of the first African American astronauts in NASA history, that challenged what he believed was possible. When Charles said he didn’t think he’d be chosen for the space shuttle program, Ron pushed back with a simple question that would change his life: “How do you know if you don’t ask?” Charles would go on to become part of the second group of space shuttle astronauts — and later the first African American to lead NASA.

items.title: AI, Corporate Responsibility & Democratic Legitimacy: Extended Q&A • Joanna Bryson • GOTO 2025
items.author: GOTO Conferences
items.content:
<article>This Q&A session was recorded at GOTO Copenhagen 2025. #GOTOcon #GOTOcph
https://gotocph.com

Joanna Bryson - Professor of Ethics and Technology, Responsible Data Science for Human Development @j2bryson 

RESOURCES
https://bsky.app/profile/j2bryson.bsky.social
https://mastodon.social/@j2bryson
https://twitter.com/j2bryson
https://www.linkedin.com/in/bryson
https://joanna-bryson.blogspot.com

Links
https://digital-markets-act.ec.europa.eu/index_en

DESCRIPTION
Joanna Bryson challenges many popular assumptions about AI and AGI. She argues that current generative AI systems are not autonomous intelligences but powerful tools that compress and reproduce human cultural knowledge at scale. While these systems can appear intelligent, they remain dependent on human-created data, corporate incentives, and carefully engineered guardrails. Bryson cautions against anthropomorphizing AI and emphasizes that many concerns about AI stem from misunderstandings about how these systems actually work.

The discussion ultimately centers on responsibility and governance. Bryson argues that transparency, accountability, and democratic oversight matter far more than debates about machine consciousness. Whether discussing AI companions, recommendation algorithms, open-source models, regulation, or AI-assisted software development, her conclusion remains consistent: technology is not destiny. Engineers, companies, policymakers, and citizens all have agency in shaping how AI is deployed, regulated, and integrated into society. [...]

items.content:

items.title: The Milwaukee Cannibal - "My Friend Dahmer" | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>The graphic novel "My Friend Dahmer” recounts the early years of a serial killer in the US. This deeply disturbing book by Derf Backderf, a school friend of Dahmer’s, delves into the depths of human madness.

Jeffrey Dahmer, one of the most feared serial killers in US history, was arrested in 1991. He had killed 17 men, most of them young, Black, and gay. When he learned of the crimes, Derf Backderf suddenly found himself confronted with his own past: at secondary school and sixth form, he had been on friendly terms with Dahmer, who was already considered an outsider at the time. To process his shock and make sense of it all, Backderf decided to write a graphic novel — a project spanning almost 20 years. "My Friend Dahmer” became one of the most significant works in the genre.

Drawing on previously unpublished archive footage and testimonies from Backderf’s friends, the documentary offers a deep insight into the long and sometimes painful process that went into creating this extraordinary book. It shows just how difficult it is to tell the story of a troubled teenager who appears to be like so many of his contemporaries, but who has a dark secret. How to maintain the necessary distance without succumbing to leniency or developing empathy for the future perpetrator? And how to confront one’s own past without harboring feelings of guilt? A difficult journey, that ultimately made Backderf one of the most famous contemporary comic artists in the US.


#documentary #dwdocumentary #dwdocs #serialkiller #jeffreydahmer
______

items.title: How H.H. Holmes Became America’s First Serial Killer
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>Long before he was hanged in 1896 for murdering his business partner, Herman Webster Mudgett, better known as Dr. Henry Howard Holmes, had built a reputation for fraud, deception, and violence. He also built an infamous "Murder Castle" in Chicago, a labyrinthine building allegedly designed to lure and kill travelers attending the 1893 World's Fair. Historians have confirmed at least nine victims, but many believe the true death toll may have been far, far higher.

On this episode of Weird History Presents: Graveyard Shift we separate the historical record from the sensational rumors surrounding H. H. Holmes, exploring the crimes, scams, and enduring mysteries that turned him into one of history's most notorious figures.

Do you believe the legends about H.H. Holmes and his"Murder Castle"? Let us know in the comments!


Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews

#</article>

items.title: Does sports betting need guardrails?
items.author: Vox
items.content:
<article>The 2026 World Cup became the biggest betting event in history, but addiction and public health experts are sounding the alarm bells on the consequences of this rampant rise in sports gambling.

Since the Supreme Court removed the federal ban on sports betting in 2018, and with the rise of prediction markets like Kalshi and Polymarket, addiction experts have seen a measurable increase in young people seeking gambling addiction treatment. A 2026 Federal Reserve report found that states that legalized sports betting are seeing lower credit scores and increased auto delinquencies, especially among those under 40.

Is it time to add guardrails to the US sports betting industry as advertising, technology, and social media converge into a 24/7 cycle of gambling exposure?

If you or a loved one are experiencing distress from gambling, you can call 1-800-MY-RESET, the national gambling helpline that gets you connected to a counselor or therapist in your area.

items.title: When Cable TV Came to a Buddhist Kingdom (full documentary) | FRONTLINE/World
items.author: FRONTLINE PBS | Official
items.content:
<article>An exploration of the impact of television on a remote Buddhist kingdom in the Himalayas. (Aired 2002)

This journalism is made possible by viewers like you. Donate to FRONTLINE now: https://bit.ly/47DFzCb

And support your local PBS station here: https://www.pbs.org/donate

In 1999, Bhutan, a Buddhist kingdom that had remained isolated in the Himalayan mountains for centuries, became the last country in the world to legalize TV.

In the years that followed, Bhutan opened itself to what some critics called “an electronic invasion” — cable television. In this FRONTLINE/World short documentary that aired in 2002, reporters Alexis Bloom and Tshewang Dendup explored the differing reactions to the advent of cable TV in the Buddhist kingdom, including the perspective of a co-owner of a cable service company, who said, “We always have to move forward.” 

“Bhutan: The Last Place” was produced by Alexis Bloom, who was also the videographer. The co-producer and videographer was Tshewang Dendup. The founder and executive producer of FRONTLINE was David Fanning. 

We are making episodes from FRONTLINE/World’s archive newly available here on YouTube and on pbs.org as part of an effort to make our past reporting widely accessible.

Originally developed by FRONTLINE producers in conjunction with public television stations KQED San Francisco and WGBH Boston, FRONTLINE/World launched in 2002 as a national public TV series that turned its lens on the global community, covering countries and cultures rarely seen on American television. The series completed its final broadcast season in 2010.

Explore more about FRONTLINE/World and this film on the legacy website for the series:
https://www.pbs.org/frontlineworld/

items.title: Demystifying Spooky Action At A Distance, with Sean Hodgman
items.author: StarTalk
items.content:
<article>Boldly Go One Step Closer To Where It All Began. Strange New Worlds S4 Streaming July 23 on Paramount+. https://www.paramountplus.com/shows/star-trek-strange-new-worlds/

T-Mobile 5G Home Internet. https://T-Mobile.com/HomeInternet

How do you get entangled particles? Neil deGrasse Tyson and comic co-host Chuck Nice unpack the experimental side of entanglement, superposition, and the quantum underpinnings of our universe with experimental physicist, Sean Hodgman.

How do you entangle particles? Can you entangle larger things, like atoms? Sean describes his job cooling helium atoms to a millionth of a degree above absolute zero to form a Bose-Einstein condensate. Learn about momentum entanglement and why entangling atoms rather than photons matters. Could entangling atoms open the window to quantum gravity?

What is superposition, really? Sean walks Chuck through quantum superpositions, Einstein’s criticisms of “spooky action at a distance,”and Bell’s Theorem. How do you actually entangle a particle, and how do you know it worked? What are the different subtypes of entanglement beyond momentum? Has studying entanglement changed how physicists understand reality? Could you ever use entanglement for faster-than-light communication? Why does the no-communication theorem say no, while quantum encryption says there's still something useful here?

The episode closes on the biggest questions: is there an upper size limit to entanglement? Could spacetime itself emerge from a network of entangled particles? Would we notice if entanglement were switched off and could quantum effects be quietly underpinning DNA stability, bird navigation, and photosynthesis?

Thanks to our Patrons Dominic B., Chirag Trasikar, Chase Dishman, Benjamin Rogers, Anthony Smith, Matt J, Bill Fisher, Gregory Shaw, Jesse Phelps, Shakil Chagani, Valentine, Sayrah rana, Linda Corbin, Kerry Gallagher, Emma Korein, Kate, Sara Drollinger, brad hodge, William Colt, Jill Campbell, Sai Dev Mahajan, Damien Hewlett, Ryan Christensen, Matthew Barrett, Pau Ferrer, Navindra Persaud, Felipe Marcondes, Deatric Wilson, Ashish Paka, Larry, BRUNO, Adán Rodríguez, Eneida Paulo, Todd P., Francesco Chionchio, Crouton, Anton Bursch, odamai, Brayden Malley, John Hamm, Tim Timrawi, Kuang kai Peter Tou, Adam Fry, Raylen, Pele_Glendale, Balázs Pető, Sergiu Calin, Shane King, Torstein Sundnes, Deonne, Mark Bailey, Rodrigo Gómez, Bri, Richard Riley, James Gray, adam, Kyle, Tam Lee, Liz Schwabedissen, Jim Dulian, and Jesse for supporting us this week.

items.title: The Berlin rental rip-off: The housing crisis boom on tiny rooms | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Just under 6 square meters for 600 euros a month. People moving to Berlin often find only overpriced, furnished short-term rooms available. It's all due to the city's housing shortage: Germany has rent cap laws, but they don't apply to temporary, furnished accommodation.

Some landlords even take it a step further. With drywall partitions, they're dividing existing apartments into multiple tiny rooms. What was once a four-bedroom apartment now has eight people crammed into it. It's often younger people moving to Berlin who face the worst of it. Now, the boroughs of Neukölln and Pankow are tightening regulations on room rentals and expanding neighborhood protection measures to crack down on the business model. Along with tenant activists, newcomers are pushing back against high rents and sketchy rental practices. The fight is on for affordable housing and the chance to build a future in Berlin.

#documentary #dwdocumentary #dwdocs #berlin #housingcrisis 
______

items.title: The mystery of the giant kelp forest - Luka Seamus Wright and Salomé Buglass
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Discover the ocean’s hidden kelp forests, and explore how these light-dependent organisms evolved to survive at dark depths.

--

Like any other plant, kelps require light to grow. Yet kelps found nearly 90 meters below the ocean's surface, where sometimes only 0.1% of sunlight can reach, grow as large as, and often even larger than, their shallow counterparts. So how have these large, light-dependent organisms adapted to thrive at such depths? Luka Seamus Wright and Salomé Buglass explore the ocean's hidden forests.

Lesson by Luka Seamus Wright and Salomé Buglass, directed by Upamanyu Bhattacharyya, Otter Studios.

items.title: The truth about gut health
items.author: Vox
items.content:
<article>Worried about the spread of the diarrhea-causing illness cyclosporiasis? A healthier gut might better prepare your body to deal with it. With all the noise of wellness experts and coaches, we decided to go directly to doctors and researchers who have been studying gut health and the human microbiome for decades. 

Gut health is everywhere right now. The hashtag #GutTok has over 6 billion views on TikTok, the global digestive health product market is worth over $60 billion, and viral trends that promise to “hack your gut” through miracle products, cleanses, and restrictive diets are all the rage. But what does gut health really mean, and what can science tell us about how to make sure our gut is in good shape?

With so many voices chiming in on gut health, pushing everything from probiotic foods and supplements to wellness shots and coffee enemas, it can be hard to tell what information is based on real research and what might just be a marketing gimmick or social media clickbait. Vox producer Benjamin Stephen spoke to doctors and visited Cedars-Sinai in Los Angeles to bust some gut health myths for science-backed advice. 

Read more about gut health and gut microbiome research:

Vox editor Katherine Courage’s book, Cultured: How Ancient Foods Can Feed Our Microbiome: https://katherinecourage.com/book/ 

Cedars-Sinai’s Human Microbiome Research Institute: https://www.cedars-sinai.edu/health-sciences-university/research/departments-institutes/medicine/human-microbiome.html 

The MAST program at Cedars-Sinai: https://csmast.com/

Trisha Pasricha’s book, You’ve Been Pooping All Wrong: https://www.trishapasricha.com/youvebeenpoopingallwrong 

Vox’s explainer video on probiotics in yogurt: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sYj09lx15ik 

Pew Research’s findings on why people look to influencers for health and wellness information: https://www.pewresearch.org/data-labs/2026/05/07/why-do-americans-get-information-from-health-and-wellness-influencers-and-how-do-they-find-them/

The Myths and Scientific Realities About Gut Microbiota: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12525918/

ABSTRACT
WebAssembly started as a technology tailored to web browsers and is becoming popular as a server-side technology as well. The next step is for Wasm to become a powerful tool for cloud-native applications. When combined with Kubernetes, WebAssembly can revolutionize application deployment, security, and resource efficiency in ways traditional containers cannot.

This talk explores why and how to leverage WebAssembly within Kubernetes environments to create scalable, high-performance, and secure applications. [...]

items.title: How One Woman's Lawsuit Abolished Slavery
items.author: PBS
items.content:

Mum Bet, a woman enslaved in Massachusetts, is terrorized by a cruel mistress until she decides to take matters into her own hands. She successfully sues for her own freedom, and the case winds up helping to abolish slavery in the state, completely.

#americanrevolution #blackhistory #history 

Declarations: Black Americans and the Revolutionary War
Follow the stories of four Black Americans as they navigate our country’s fight for independence and their individual pursuits of freedom. Discover the stories of James Lafayette, who served as a double agent for the Patriots and helped bring down the British at Yorktown; Harry Washington, who was enslaved by George Washington and escaped Mount Vernon to join the British troops; Elizabeth Freeman, previously known as Mum Bet, who sued one of the most prominent patriots in Massachusetts while living under his roof; and Abraham Peyton Skipwith, an enslaved man who used his proximity to power and his unusual literacy  to petition for his own freedom and become one of the first free Black landowners in Richmond, VA.</article>

items.title: Couchsurfing in Argentina: Soccer fever, tango and wild Patagonia | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Argentina is in turmoil, as President Milei reshapes the country with radical austerity measures. Subsidies are being cut, the state is shrinking, and reforms are dividing society. What does this mean for the people living there?

The German journalist and travel writer Stephan Orth visited Argentina and travelled through the country couchsurfing. What he found was a divided country going through a tumultuous time: Since Javier Milei took office, Argentina has been experiencing a period of radical transformation. Subsidies are being cut, the public sector drastically downsized, labor rights weakened, and environmental protection laws rolled back — all in the name of a neoliberal shock therapy that has deeply shaken the country.

But despite the crises, Argentinians have not lost their joie de vivre. Buenos Aires remains crazy about soccer, especially during the Superclásico rivalry between Boca Juniors and River Plate. In Rosario, north of Buenos Aires, everyday life is as hectic as ever. The film shows how the economic crisis is affecting people's daily lives in the cities. Meanwhile, in Patagonia, the stunning natural landscape attracts millions of visitors every year. But the country’s transformation can be felt even there.

Between soccer, everyday life in times of crisis, and the sweeping scenery of the Andes, Argentina is a country in transition — divided, contradictory, and full of energy.


#documentary #dwdocumentary #dwdocs #argentina

items.title: The Miserable End Of Henry VIII | Last Days
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>Ravaged by illness, consumed by paranoia, and fully aware that his reign was coming to an end, King Henry VIII spent his last days doing everything he could to protect his son Edward's future ascent to the throne. But as they often did with Henry... things got bloody and people got executed.

LAST DAYS is Weird History’s prestige format series focusing on final chapters, deaths, downfalls and conclusions. In this premiere episode, we examine the shocking true story behind Henry VIII's last execution, the brutal politics of the Tudor dynasty, and the dark final days of one of history's most notorious monarchs.

Do you think Henry was paranoid? Or was he right to be worried about Edward's reign? Let un know in the comments.

(Much like real history, this video was produced entirely by human beings.)

items.title: Israel's era of military rule | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>After the founding of the State of Israel, Zvi Elpeleg served as ‘military governor for the control of Arab civilians.’

For decades, Zvi Elpeleg was regarded as a respected public servant and family man. But this image starts to crumble when his granddaughter, filmmaker Danel Elpeleg, starts looking more closely into his past. 
Based on private letters, official documents, and conversations with people who knew him, a complex story unfolds — one that is both personal and political. The filmmaker not only uncovers a hidden family secret, but also confronts the moral gray zones of Israeli history. How far does the power of the state extend into the lives of its citizens? How does this power shape people's identities across generations? 
"The Governor” is more than a portrait of a man — it is a critical reflection on control, responsibility, and the mechanisms of state authority. With haunting archival footage, personal interviews, and an intimate narrative style, the result is a multi-layered work that raises questions about power, morality, and memory.

items.title: The Science of Sleep, with Matt Walker
items.author: StarTalk
items.content:
<article>Boldly Go One Step Closer To Where It All Began. Strange New Worlds S4 Streaming July 23 on Paramount+. https://www.paramountplus.com/shows/star-trek-strange-new-worlds/

What’s the real difference between dreaming and waking consciousness?  Neil deGrasse Tyson and co-host Chuck Nice and Gary O’Reilly sit down with sleep expert Matt Walker to answer fan questions about the most mysterious thing humans do every single night: sleeping & dreaming. What’s the science behind your sleep paralysis demon? 

Why did evolution keep sleep? We explore why sleep stuck around and what the latest in sleep research looks like. Could genetic short sleepers hold the key to "zip-filing" sleep into fewer hours? Could we ever reprogram our genetics to not need sleep at all? We discuss if this is possible and whether losing the ability to dream would be detrimental. 

What does dreaming actually do? Matt explains how REM sleep produces informational alchemy and why dreaming may be the biological basis of human creativity and overnight emotional therapy. Neil shares how he slept eleven hours the night after 9/11, and Matt connects that to the brain's drive for more sleep when processing large amounts of information.

If the dreaming brain can generate an entire universe without sensory input, what does that say about waking consciousness? Is waking life itself just a controlled hallucination tethered to time? We break down the difference between waking and dreaming as well as the brain's cleansing mechanism: the gylmphatic system. How does its failure to clear toxic proteins connect directly to Alzheimer's disease? The episode covers sleep stages and cycles, why sleep inertia makes waking from deep sleep so hard, and what parasomnias (like sleepwalking and sleep paralysis) reveal about the porous boundary between waking and sleeping consciousness. Plus, a question about how astronauts’ sleep is impacted by being in space. 

Thanks to our Patrons Rhagor, Beans, Eric Grant, DOCTOR FN ASTRONAUT, Richard Thompson, Niclas Larsson, Cathy Hayes, SunshineKat Snyder, Brandon, Matt Caldwell, Tim Davies, M.Victoria Delpiano, Heyo13579, Erin McCullough, Shalon Sorensen, Kaydence Johnson, RJ, Dave, Sindre Skybert, Mr. V, Neon lion Blacklight cozylazy, Mary Mitchell, Trip Ni Selo, S. H., Cloud3X, P.M., Dallas, Dan, Getyoducksinarow, David S, Michael Koenig, Jason A, Reg Anderson, Sean Marien, Adeel Rahman, joe hoffmann, Ronald Merrifield, Jesse Miner, David Pazer, Toshi Jordan, Pat Voss, Nicholas Wiseman, Antoine Portes, Colin, David Crouse, Sander van Liempd, Wendy R., J P, Kenneth Cook, Joey, Fixxxer331, Nathan Hinote, Hara Kannajosyula, Heath Horne, Pixtle, Brett M Garabedian, Ryan Alba, Paul Tindall, Tony Stewart, and Stephanie Moore for supporting us this week.

items.title: How to Be Right and Lose Everything
items.author: Patrick Boyle
items.content:
<article>Get an exclusive 15% discount on Saily data plans! Use code BOYLE at checkout. Download Saily app or go to https://saily.com/boyle

South Korea has the best performing stock market in the world for the second year running — and it's also in the middle of one of the worst bear markets on earth. The KOSPI is down around 27% from its June peak, more than 1.2 million retail accounts have been hit with margin calls, and hundreds of thousands of Korean investors have been wiped out entirely. But this isn't a story about meme stocks or worthless companies. Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix are enormously profitable businesses at the center of the global AI boom, and the traders buying them were right about the trend. In this video I look at how a national stock index became a two-stock bet on artificial intelligence, how single-stock leveraged ETFs turned ordinary volatility into a mechanical feedback loop, why Korea's retail "ants" took on so much leverage in the first place, and what Victor Haghani's famous biased-coin experiment tells us about how you can be completely right about a market and still lose everything.

items.title: The Real Story of Van Gogh's Ear | Full Episode | Secrets of the Dead
items.author: PBS
items.content:
<article>Watch more: https://to.pbs.org/3mHxfbj | #SecretsDeadPBS
The moment when Vincent van Gogh looked into a mirror, held up a blade and cut into his own ear defines his turbulent life and art. But what did he really do on the night of December 23, 1888, in the town of Arles? What was his state of mind? No one has been clear on the details... until now. Bernadette Murphy, a researcher living in Provence, has uncovered definitive evidence.

Even those with a scant knowledge of art know about the moment when artist Vincent van Gogh cut into his ear. The deed was dramatized by Irving Stone in his best-selling novel “Lust for Life,” and portrayed vividly by Kirk Douglas in the 1956 film.

But did Stone get it right? What did van Gogh really do on the fateful night in southern France? Afterwards, there was a successful effort by his family to play down the event. His friend, artist Paul Gauguin, who was present, gave conflicting accounts. Still others tried to profit from his local infamy. Generations have theorized about what really happened, but until now, no one has unearthed the true details. [Originally aired in 2016]

Van Gogh's Ear | Secrets of the Dead

items.title: What makes us happier? - The formula for happiness | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Why are the Finns the undisputed world champions when it comes to happiness? They have topped the United Nations’ "World Happiness Report” eight times in a row. Is it nature, saunas, tango, or karaoke that brings the Finns so much happiness?

Can we learn to be happy? Or does happiness reside in our genes? At first glance, the results of the World Happiness Report don’t seem to fit the cold, dark North at all. Winters are harsh. Many Finns have an alcohol problem. Russia is a direct threat. The economy is stagnating. Yet the vast majority of Finns feel happy. Finnish actress Meri Koivisto sets out to discover what the formula for happiness in the far north could be.

items.title: Homelessness in Berlin | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Anyone walking through Berlin today is confronted with the reality of the city's housing crisis. More than 50,000 people currently lack a permanent place to call home. The reasons for this reality vary from poverty and sudden eviction to personal misfortune, debt, or addiction. While some find temporary shelter in emergency housing, around 6,000 individuals are left to survive entirely on the streets, setting up makeshift beds under bridges or in parks. But behind these staggering statistics are human beings who refuse to be defined solely by their circumstances.

items.author: Bloomberg Originals
items.content:

That unexpected resilience of markets has many explanations, but it’s forcing a bigger question: does the world simply wait for the critical waterway to normalize, or start building alternative routes, inventories and supply systems to hedge against future risks?

items.title: French Resistance vs Waffen-SS: The Battle For The South
items.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
items.content:
<article>On June 6, 1944, as Allied forces landed at Normandy, the notorious 2nd Panzer Waffen-SS Division Das Reich began a bloodstained march north from Montauban. Hardened by the brutal warfare of the Eastern Front, these elite forces met fierce guerrilla resistance from the French Maquis and British SOE agents. In retaliation, the SS unleashed unparalleled terror across the French countryside, leading to the horrific atrocities at Tulle and the total annihilation of Oradour-sur-Glane. This documentary uncovers the chilling testimonies, tactical maneuvers, and war crimes that forever scarred occupied France during the Second World War.

items.title: The Universe Has Been Expanding Since the Big Bang. Just Not How We Thought.
items.author: PBS Space Time
items.content:
<article>Check out the Space Time Merch Store
https://www.pbsspacetime.com/shop

Last year, the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument—DESI—made headlines around the world. Its enormous map of the Universe hinted that dark energy—the mysterious something causing the expansion of the Universe to accelerate—might not actually be constant. Last month, another giant cosmology experiment released its final results. The Dark Energy Survey. DES. Its results are intriguing. But did they confirm the DESI measurement? We’ll, let’s see, but there’s also a much more interesting question—Why are completely different scientific studies so much more powerful in combination than any one study on its own? The answer brings us to the heart of what science really is.

items.title: Nigeria’s mysterious art treasures | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>The first major city in sub-Saharan Africa was founded in what is now Nigeria. Ilé-Ifè, the sacred city of the Yoruba, was a center of African civilization. Until its decline.

When the German collector and African explorer Leo Frobenius became the first European to reach the city of Ilé-Ifè in south-western Nigeria in 1910, the realistic portrait art he encountered led him to believe he had stumbled upon traces of the lost continent of Atlantis. A golden age in the African rainforest? In the era of imperialism and colonialism, nobody in Europe was willing to believe it. Yet in recent years, several research teams have examined the city’s rise, its trade relations, the daily lives of its citizens and, ultimately, its decline in greater detail.

Whilst a team led by the Frenchman Gérard Chouin is developing a chronology of the city based on its pottery and tiled floors, Nigerian archaeologist Babatunde Babalola takes us on a treasure hunt around a glass factory dating from the early heyday of Ilé-Ifès. The entire area is littered with beads. A monopoly on the production and trade of glass beads became the economic backbone of Ilé-Ifès. Soon, the city became the blueprint for the founding of further kingdoms.

But around the year 1400 AD, the city began to decline. Was a major plague epidemic responsible? Scholars disagree. At the very latest, however, with the arrival of the Portuguese in 1471 and the onset of the transatlantic slave trade, the city’s glory days were finally over.

items.title: We Didn't Predict the Sun Would Do This
items.author: Astrum
items.content:
<article>Why was the Sun’s recent solar maximum twice as strong as predicted? In 2024-2025, the Sun entered a fiery active phase that completely defied expectations. But thanks to NASA's Parker Solar Probe, we now have observations taken from inside the Sun's atmosphere, which could help explain what’s powering the unusual eruptions. What’s really going on inside our star? And should we be worried here on Earth?

items.title: America Wanted Them Back as Slaves. This British Lord Said No
items.author: PBS
items.content:
<article>Watch the full documentary: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fMpYozsIyX0

In 1775 Lord Dunmore’s proclamation promised freedom to any enslaved person who would come fight for the British. Thousands of people answered the call. But at the end of the war, the Americans tried to insist that all the enslaved who escaped, must be returned. Lord Dunmore secretly sent ships with thousands of Black British soldiers to Nova Scotia.

#americanrevolution #blackhistory #history 

Declarations: Black Americans and the Revolutionary War
Follow the stories of four Black Americans as they navigate our country’s fight for independence and their individual pursuits of freedom. Discover the stories of James Lafayette, who served as a double agent for the Patriots and helped bring down the British at Yorktown; Harry Washington, who was enslaved by George Washington and escaped Mount Vernon to join the British troops; Elizabeth Freeman, previously known as Mum Bet, who sued one of the most prominent patriots in Massachusetts while living under his roof; and Abraham Peyton Skipwith, an enslaved man who used his proximity to power and his unusual literacy  to petition for his own freedom and become one of the first free Black landowners in Richmond, VA.</article>

items.title: Muscle mania and the cult of masculinity - When the body becomes an obsession | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Until recently, the use of anabolic steroids was the privilege of elite athletes. But the use of performance-enhancing drugs among young men has now reached alarming proportions.

A powerful documentary about a phenomenon that’s increasingly alarming: young men’s fixation on the perfect body, and their growing use of performance-enhancing substances to achieve it. What was once reserved for professional elite sports is now widespread among teenagers and young men with bodybuilding ambitions. The consequences are serious.

The film radically challenges prevailing male beauty ideals and delves deep into the mechanisms of a global muscle-building industry. Conceived of as an investigative field study, the film reveals the social, psychological, and physical risks associated with the relentless pursuit of corporeal perfection.

By making his own vulnerability the starting point, the filmmaker opens an intimate window into an often-invisible world — while simultaneously exposing the scale of an emerging public health crisis. The film’s perspective is as unsparing as it is sensitive, and shows a system that standardizes bodies, pushes boundaries, and drives young men into a dangerous race against themselves.

items.title: The Dark Life of Aleister Crowley
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>From founding a new spiritual philosophy to influencing modern occultism, yoga, and pop culture, Aleister Crowley built a legacy that still sparks fascination and debate more than a century later.

In this episode of Weird History Presents: Graveyard Shift, we attempt to uncover the truth behind Crowley's dark reputation as "the wickedest man in the world", his bizarre rituals, infamous scandals, and the beliefs that made him one of the most mysterious figures of the 20th century. Was he an evil mastermind, a misunderstood visionary, or something in between?

What's the craziest story you ever heard about Aleister Crowley? Let us know in the comments.

Be sure to subscribe to the Weird History Newsletter: https://bit.ly/WeirdHistoryNews

#aleistercrowley #occult #weirdhistory</article>

items.title: Inside a Changing Nation | Vietnam: Looking for Home (full documentary) | FRONTLINE/World
items.author: FRONTLINE PBS | Official
items.content:
<article>Decades after the Vietnam War shattered his childhood, journalist Nguyen Qui Duc traveled to his homeland and documented the changes he witnessed in the country. (Aired 2003)

This journalism is made possible by viewers like you. Donate to FRONTLINE now: https://bit.ly/47DFzCb

And support your local PBS station here: https://www.pbs.org/donate

Decades ago, the Vietnam War shattered Nguyen Qui Duc’s childhood. Over the years, Nguyen, who came to the U.S. as a refugee, returned to his homeland as a journalist, reporting on the country’s culture and establishing connections with writers and artists living there. In 2003, he documented a journey through Vietnam for FRONTLINE/World, looking, he said, “for home, for a bit of myself, for a country that always exists in my memory.”

As Nguyen traveled to different parts of the country in “Vietnam: Looking for Home,” he shared his reflections about the nation: “It’s easy to remember Vietnam has a habit of adopting and resisting changes. The culture, the people, will survive.”

Several years after “Vietnam: Looking for Home” aired, Nguyen returned to Vietnam, this time for good. He filed occasional dispatches with FRONTLINE and FRONTLINE/World, and opened an influential arts and culture salon that pushed the boundaries of Vietnam’s communist government. Nguyen described the salon as “a gallery, an event space, a meeting point for creative and unorthodox people and a comfort space for expats.” He ran it until his death in 2023.  

Originally developed by FRONTLINE producers in conjunction with public television stations KQED San Francisco and WGBH Boston, FRONTLINE/World launched in 2002 as a national public TV series that turned its lens on the global community, covering countries and cultures rarely seen on American television. The series completed its final broadcast season in 2010.

We are making episodes from FRONTLINE/World’s archive newly available here on YouTube and on pbs.org as part of an effort to make our past reporting widely accessible.

“Vietnam: Looking for Home” was reported by Nguyen Qui Duc. It was produced by Camille Servan-Schreiber, who was also the videographer. The founder and executive producer of FRONTLINE was David Fanning. 

Explore more about FRONTLINE/World and this film on the legacy website for the series:

items.title: The Bloodiest Months: How The Allies Finally Entered Germany
items.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
items.content:

From the costly mountains of Italy and the monsoons of Burma to the bloody beaches of Iwo Jima and the freezing forests of the Ardennes, this premium documentary explores the final, brutal chapters of WW2. items.title: Would You Really Want to Know if You’re in a Simulation? | Cosmic Queries #113
items.author: StarTalk
items.content:
<article>T-Mobile 5G Home Internet. https://T-Mobile.com/HomeInternet

Boldly Go One Step Closer To Where It All Began. Strange New Worlds S4 Streaming July 23 on Paramount+. https://www.paramountplus.com/shows/star-trek-strange-new-worlds/

Is there no single shared "now" for everyone in the universe? Neil deGrasse Tyson and comic co-host Paul Mecurio dive into another grab bag of fan questions about superconducting asteroids, what memories are made of, and if you could ever experience pure unwarped spacetime.  

First a question from Interstellar: what would the crewmembers on Miller’s Planet see if they looked up at their crewmate through a telescope? Neil explains how light arrives at different paces depending on your position in a gravitational well. If we're living in a simulation, would you want to know? Is randomness in the universe actually just sloppy programming? Learn about chaos as a precise mathematical phenomenon and how consistent laws guide the universe. 

Could an asteroid become a superconductor in the near-absolute-zero cold of space? What would happen to ocean tides if the Moon left orbit? Are human memories really just atoms, and what does that mean for people experiencing memory loss or dementia? Is there a "purpose" to a black hole, or is that the wrong question entirely?

The episode closes on some of the deepest physics of the hour: is there anywhere in the universe free from all gravitational influence, where you could experience pure, unwarped spacetime? Neil explains why gravity technically never reaches zero, no matter how far you travel. We question whether information that falls into a black hole could actually be reconstructed from the particles at its surface and explore the sponge-like structure of galaxies across the universe.

Thanks to our Patrons Alex Sadashiv Nayak, Raviteja K, Lian Zhalka, Thomas Davis, Alex Yumashev, Charles Koehl, Matthew Maldonado, Aleksandre Khatiskatsi, Grig Coker, Matthew Ardebi, Alex Sadashiv Nayak, Raviteja K, Lian Zhalka, Thomas Davis, Alex Yumashev, Charles Koehl, Matthew Maldonado, Aleksandre Khatiskatsi, Grig Coker, Matthew Ardebili, Japeth Mitchell, Gaaary, Ian Patton, Casey Steelspine, Veninator, Hannu Latvakoski, Santiago Aguirre, Jonathan Caples, Ryan Wetmore, Dan Lepping, Chris Frank Betz, William Massar, Jason Durden, Jenny Patton, LiveAlive42, Kesh Iyer, scott ezell, Jesse Jensen, Javier E. items.title: The revival of Spain's dictator Franco? | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Ninety years ago, a coup against the Spanish Republic led to Francisco Franco's dictatorship. Today, many people of all ages in Spain are romanticizing that regime, a trend fueled by the far-right political party Vox.

After Franco's death in 1975, Spain's democracy was established based on the Amnesty Law of 1977, which shielded Franco-era agents and officials from prosecution. But victims are still demanding justice and compensation. Today, there are two opposing narratives: One is based on a duty to tell the truth, while the other uses nostalgia to revive a former authoritarian system of government. Between the ruling left-wing coalition, right wing parties and the far right, there appears to be no path forward to reconcile conflicting views of Franco's legacy.
Against the backdrop of this political divide, even some young people long for the old order under Franco. Right-wing populists are exploiting this trend to once again call democracy into question — especially the far-right party Vox, whose influence is growing among voters.

items.title: When did we start using passports? - Kristin Surak
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Trace the history of passports to find out why we need these documents and what challenges travelers face within this system.

--

Passports are not the first form of travel documents: in Ancient Egypt, travelers carried “letters of safe conduct” issued by rulers; in Han China and Tudor England, foreigners were issued travel passes. However, for much of human history, borders have been relatively fluid, and travel lacked bureaucratic requirements. So, what changed? Kristin Surak details the history of the passport system.

Lesson by Kristin Surak, directed by Tomás Pichardo-Espaillat.

items.title: Reshaping the future of criminal investigations | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Forensic science is advancing rapidly. What began over a century ago with fingerprint analysis has evolved into a high-tech discipline. Today, even the smallest traces of DNA can help investigators identify a suspected killer. 

For criminals, the walls are closing in. Crime scenes can now be scanned with lasers and reconstructed in 3D, allowing investigators to revisit every detail and test how a crime may have unfolded. The same technology is increasingly being used to digitally scan victims, preserving evidence long after a body is gone. 

From virtual crime scenes to microscopic DNA, modern forensics is making it harder than ever to get away with murder.

items.title: Beyond Sonic Pi: Tau5 & the Art of Coding with AI • Sam Aaron • YOW! 2025
items.author: GOTO Conferences
items.content:
<article>This presentation was recorded at YOW! Australia 2025. #GOTOcon #YOW
https://yowcon.com

Sam Aaron - Live Coding Musician and Creator of Sonic Pi @SamAaron 

RESOURCES
https://www.patreon.com/samaaron
https://bsky.app/profile/samaaron.bsky.social
https://twitter.com/samaaron
https://github.com/samaaron
https://linkedin.com/in/samaaron

Links
https://sonic-pi.net
https://sonic-pi.net/supersonic/demo.html

ABSTRACT
For over a decade, Sonic Pi has been used in classrooms around the world to teach programming through music. Students learn sequencing, loops, data structures, and algorithms while coding beats, basslines, and shimmering synths. At the same time, professional musicians have taken Sonic Pi to stages and concert halls, using programming itself as an instrument of musical expression.

Now the story continues with Tau5, a new live coding system focused on collaboration. In music, concurrency, distribution, and fault tolerance aren’t just safety nets - they are uncompromising demands. When harnessed, they can be transformed into powerful creative possibilities for human expression and connection.

Collaboration isn’t limited to humans. Tau5 also experiments with AI as a creative partner, asking not what machines can replace, but how they might improvise with us.

This keynote reflects on the lessons these explorations in music and code can teach us about collaboration, creativity, and education in the age of AI. Along the way, we’ll explore concurrency, rhythm, and the subtle but critical difference between working at the same time and working truly in time.

Don't just pattern match - rhythm catch! [...]

items.title: Was Agincourt The Most BRUTAL Medieval Battle in History?
items.author: History Hit
items.content:
<article>How did an army of English peasant archers defeat some of France's greatest warriors? 

On October 25th 1415, Henry V of England took on the mighty French forces at the Battle of Agincourt. Despite being heavily outnumbered, his troops won. It would become one of the most legendary victories in English history - a tale of bravery and triumph against all the odds, inspiring everyone from Shakespeare to Churchill to Kenneth Branagh. But how much of the story we know today is true, and how much has been twisted to suit later politics? What really happened at Agincourt?

items.title: The Real Story Of Genghis Khan And The Mongol Empire
items.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
items.content:
<article>Genghis Khan is often remembered as a brutal barbarian who butchered millions, but the true history of the Mongol Emperor is far more complex. This premium documentary traces his extraordinary journey from Temujin, a starving illiterate outcast, to the "Universal King" who conquered the largest contiguous land empire in history. Through original sources like The Secret History of the Mongols, we explore his revolutionary military tactics, the psychological warfare that terrified nations, the brutal siege of Beijing, and the enduring mystery surrounding his final resting place, which remains undiscovered to this day.

items.title: Forensics 3.0 - How modern crime labs are closing in on criminals | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Forensic science is advancing rapidly. What began 100 years ago with fingerprint analysis has evolved into a high-tech discipline. Today, even the smallest traces of DNA can help investigators identify a suspected killer.

For criminals, the walls are closing in. Crime scenes are now scanned with lasers and reconstructed virtually, allowing investigators to revisit every detail and reexamine how a crime may have unfolded. The same technology is increasingly being used on corpses, which can also be digitally scanned and analyzed.

But artificial intelligence is creating new challenges for investigators. Criminals are using AI tools to manipulate video and audio recordings, producing highly realistic fake content. The software needed for such alterations is freely available online, raising serious concerns for criminal investigations and the integrity of evidence.

The State Criminal Police Office (LKA) in Wiesbaden and forensic specialists in Frankfurt are considered leaders in the development of these new investigative methods. With increasingly sophisticated forensic technologies, investigators are getting closer than ever to perpetrators.

items.title: We’ve Been Wrong About the Trojan Horse
items.author: Weird History
items.content:
<article>The legendary tale of the Trojan War has captivated people for thousands of years, filled with Greek gods, epic battles, mysterious prophecies, and the famous giant wooden horse said to have brought down the city of Troy. But was the Trojan Horse real, or is it one of history's greatest myths?

On this episode of Weird History, we examine the possibility that historians have misunderstood the Trojan Horse all along. Was it truly a massive wooden horse hiding Greek soldiers, or could it have been something entirely different?

Do you think there was really a Trojan Horse? Let us know in the comments.

(Much like real history, this video was produced entirely by human beings.)

items.title: On the smugglers’ trail - Making billions from tiny eels | DW Documentary
items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>The European eel is threatened with extinction. But it is also a highly sought-after commodity on the global market. The trade in young eels is booming. And it’s increasingly controlled by organized crime.

The film "Billion Dollar Babies — The Big Business of Eel Trafficking" tells the story of a multi-billion-dollar illegal trade, centered on one of nature’s most enigmatic creatures. Born in the Sargasso Sea near the Bermuda Triangle, the eel begins its journey of up to 5,000 kilometers to Europe — as a translucent ‘willow-leaf’ larva known as a glass eel. To this day, it remains unclear exactly how the European eel reproduces. Commercial breeding isn’t possible: every eel consumed comes from the wild.
The documentary follows the trail of a global network of smugglers, middlemen and high-ranking triad bosses. The investigative journey takes viewers from European fishing grounds via Paris to Hong Kong and on to mainland China. It extends as far as Haiti and examines the role that the lucrative eel smuggling trade may have played in the circumstances surrounding the assassination of the Haitian president.
What begins as an investigation into a supposed niche area of the fishing industry turns into a shocking exposé of globalized environmental crime - with profit margins comparable to those of cocaine smuggling. The eel has become one of the most smuggled animals in the world.
With impressive cinematography and exclusive access to investigators, insiders and key figures, the film reveals how the pursuit of profit is quietly driving an ecological disaster.

items.author: DW Documentary
items.content:
<article>Right-wing extremist parties are much less popular among women than men, but certain organizations are looking to change that. Far-right organizations for young women are growing in Europe and they're demanding the deportation of "millions of people." 

Observers warn that groups like Lukreta in Germany and Nemesis in France are pushing "remigration", the mass deportation of migrants, by exploiting cases of gender-based violence, even when victims' families reject the political publicity. Extremism researcher Juliane Lang says that representing male violence as something that is imported is a classic rightwing extremist strategy and distracts from systemic internal issues.

items.title: Is Russia Actually Losing?
items.author: Patrick Boyle
items.content:
<article>Go to http://ground.news/pb for a better way to stay informed. Subscribe for 40% off unlimited access to worldwide coverage through my link.

Russia's war economy is running out of road. Ukrainian drone strikes have knocked out roughly a third of Russia's oil refining capacity, forcing an energy superpower to ration petrol at home and import gasoline from India — while Crimea sits under a state of emergency with the air-raid sirens switched off so the tourists aren't disturbed. In this video we look at what the numbers actually say: a contracting economy despite record military spending, a National Wealth Fund down from 6.5% of GDP to 1.8%, a first-quarter budget deficit that blew through the entire year's target in ninety days, and a banking system quietly commandeered to keep the tank factories running. We look at how China has turned the "no limits" partnership into a very one-sided arrangement, why Europe's largest rearmament since the Cold War sent defence stocks down, whether Europe actually needs expensive American fighter jets or is simply paying tribute to Washington — and what the Russian endgame realistically looks like, according to a Russian billionaire who is not a dissident. Featuring the Kiel Institute's "Endgame" report, Tucker Carlson's demon, and the parade Vladimir Putin had to get Zelensky's permission to hold.

Most of the economic figures in this video came from the Kiel Institute paper - Endgame: Russia’s war economy hits its limits.  https://www.kielinstitut.de/publications/news/endgame-russias-war-economy-hits-its-limits/

items.title: Inside Ravensbrück: The Forgotten All-Female Concentration Camp Of WW2
items.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
items.content:
<article>In 1939, Heinrich Himmler established Ravensbrück, the Third Reich’s only concentration camp built exclusively for women and children. Located just north of Berlin, this massive 9-square-kilometer complex became a living hell for over 130,000 prisoners, including Jewish families, political dissidents, and members of the French resistance. Through extraordinary firsthand testimonies from the last survivors and rare clandestine drawings, this premium documentary uncovers the shocking reality of everyday survival, brutal female SS guards, horrific medical experiments, and the incredible, secret acts of defiance that resisted the Nazi war machine until liberation in 1945.

As tensions rise and divisions deepen over protest, principle, and truth, BREAKING the DEADLOCK: How to Fix an Election confronts what it will take to keep democracy alive.</article>

items.author: GOTO Conferences
items.content:
<article>This presentation was recorded at GOTO Copenhagen 2025. #GOTOcon #GOTOcph
https://gotocph.com

Sara Bergman - Co-Author of "Building Green Software" & Software Engineer at Microsoft @sarabergman1396 

RESOURCES
https://bsky.app/profile/saraemilybergman.bsky.social
http://twitter.com/SaraEbergman
https://github.com/SaraEmilyBergman
https://www.linkedin.com/in/sara-bergman1

Links
https://ourworldindata.org/energy
https://ourworldindata.org/electricity-mix
https://blog.cloudflare.com/designing-edge-servers-with-arm-cpus
https://amperecomputing.com/assets/amadeus_case_study_8794122fe5.pdf
https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/azure-cobalt-100-based-virtual-machines-are-now-generally-available
https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/compute/introducing-googles-new-arm-based-cpu
https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/performance-improvements-in-net-9
https://devblogs.microsoft.com/dotnet/performance-improvements-in-net-9/#arm64
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/core/compatibility/sdk/6.0/path-x64-emulated
https://github.com/danielmarbach/beyondsimplebenchmarks
https://blogs.windows.com/windowsdeveloper/2025/04/14/github-actions-now-supports-windows-on-arm-runners-for-all-public-repos
https://github.blog/news-insights/product-news/arm64-on-github-actions-powering-faster-more-efficient-build-systems

ABSTRACT
As the tech industry faces increasing pressure to reduce its environmental footprint, software engineers are well positioned to drive change—not just through efficient code, but by making smarter architectural choices.

This talk is a practical guide for engineers looking to port .NET services across architectures, particularly toward ARM-based platforms, to unlock energy efficiency and performance gains.

We’ll walk through the real-world journey of moving services from traditional x86 and x64 to ARM, highlighting the common roadblocks and how to overcome them.

This session is not just about theory—it’s a how-to for engineers who want to make their services greener without sacrificing quality or velocity. Whether you're working on microservices, APIs, or backend systems, you’ll leave a notion of what to watch out for and a clear understanding of the potential wins—both for your service and the planet. [...]

A family-friendly wildlife movie that takes viewers on an unforgettable journey down the mighty Colorado River. The film beautifully showcases the region’s abundant wildlife, confronts the ecological impacts of dams and water overuse, and advocates for better management of the river. Made in association with Natives Outdoors, the movie incorporates an indigenous storyline and is told through the perspective of some of the Southwest’s most charming characters… such as industrious beavers, bugling bull elk, and soaring condors. Between its legendary landscapes and fascinating wildlife, The American Southwest aims to endear millions of people to better cherish, love, and conserve this unique region.</article>

items.title: Democracy Under Fire | Breaking the Deadlock: How to Fix an Election | Full Episode
items.author: PBS
items.content:
<article>Official website: https://to.pbs.org/3ZsIPN5 | #DeadlockPBS

What happens when America’s most fundamental values are put to the test?

In Breaking the Deadlock: How to Fix an Election, moderator Aaron Tang (UC Davis School of Law) guides a diverse group of influential voices through an unfolding hypothetical crisis they’ve never seen before. What begins as a localized infrastructure threat quickly escalates, spilling into a high-stakes national moment involving an upcoming election, voting rights, and questions of national security. As the scenario intensifies, panelists confront whether core democratic principles — voting power, free speech, and the right to protest — can withstand the pressure. Turning to the future, participants share their vision for the next 250 years of American democracy and seek something often in short supply: common ground.

This program was recorded live at The New York Historical, collecting birthday wishes for the USA as it prepares to celebrate its 250th birthday. Learn more at www.nyhistory.org

Funding for BREAKING the DEADLOCK was made possible in part by PBS viewers.

For bios of the panelists, please visit 
https://www.pbs.org/show/deadlock/

items.title: Caught in India’s AIDS Crisis | India: The Sex Workers (full documentary) | FRONTLINE/World
items.author: FRONTLINE PBS | Official
items.content:
<article>A 2004 examination of India’s AIDS crisis through the eyes of girls and young women sold into sex work, originally aired as part of the FRONTLINE/World series. (Aired 2004)

This journalism is made possible by viewers like you. Donate to FRONTLINE now: https://bit.ly/47DFzCb

And support your local PBS station here: https://www.pbs.org/donate

Starting with “India: The Sex Workers,” we are making episodes from FRONTLINE/World’s archive newly available here on YouTube and on pbs.org as part of an effort to make our past reporting widely accessible.

Originally developed by FRONTLINE producers in conjunction with public television stations KQED San Francisco and WGBH Boston, FRONTLINE/World launched in 2002 as a national public TV series that turned its lens on the global community, covering countries and cultures rarely seen on American television. The series completed its final broadcast season in 2010.

At the time this FRONTLINE/World documentary was filmed, more than two million women and girls were working in India’s sex trade, and being forced to navigate their country’s AIDS crisis. 

With more than 4.5 million HIV infections across India at the time, often, the women and girls’ only defense against HIV was convincing men to wear condoms. But frequently, the documentary revealed, the men would refuse.

As she reported this story, Raney Aronson-Rath, who is now FRONTLINE’s editor-in-chief and executive producer, found girls as young as 12 years old who had been sold into prostitution by their families. She also found organizations that were trying to fight back and help India’s sex workers protect themselves from HIV, including a sex workers union, a group of peer educators, and an AIDS prevention group.

“India: The Sex Workers” was A Little Rain Productions for FRONTLINE/World. It was reported and produced by Raney Aronson-Rath. It was edited by Seth Bomse. The founder and executive producer of FRONTLINE was David Fanning. 

Explore more about FRONTLINE/World and this film on the legacy website for the series:

items.title: The Universe is Stranger Than We Could’ve Imagined, with Mordecai-Mark Mac Low
items.author: StarTalk
items.content:
<article>T-Mobile 5G Home Internet. https://T-Mobile.com/HomeInternet

What type of planets orbit black holes? Neil deGrasse Tyson and comic co-host Negin Farsad sit down with Mordecai-Mark Mac Low to crack open the mysteries of galaxy collisions, dark matter, and the massive planetary systems around black holes. 

We explore the catalogue of peculiar galaxies that got astronomers thinking about what makes some galaxies weird. Mordecai explains why actual collisions are rare unless you’re a gas cloud headed toward another gas cloud. We talk about active galactic nuclei and how black holes generate the brightest objects in the universe. 

Learn how the Earth formed and how accretion disks keep cropping up in different places in the universe. What are planetary systems like around supermassive black holes? Mordecai tells us about Jupiter-mass rocky planets around black holes. Could they hold the potential for life or would they be hostile to biology? A question about Jupiter's gravity leads to the story of Shoemaker-Levy 9, the first observed comet impact in our solar system. Fan questions push further: could Jupiter ignite into a star if it gained enough mass? How is dark matter built into galaxy simulations? How long does it actually take to form a star versus a planet, and could simulations ever reveal whether large moons like ours are common? 

Thanks to our Patrons Alex, Sadashiv Nayak, Raviteja K, Lian Zhalka, Thomas Davis, Alex Yumashev, Charles Koehl, Matthew Maldonado, Aleksandre Khatiskatsi, Grig Coker, Matthew Ardebili, Japeth Mitchell, Gaaary, Ian Patton, Casey Steelspine, Veninator, Hannu Latvakoski, Santiago Aguirre, Jonathan Caples, Ryan Wetmore, Dan Lepping, Chris, Frank Betz, William Massar, Jason Durden, Jenny Patton, LiveAlive42, Kesh Iyer, scott ezell, Jesse Jensen, Javier E. Gonzalez, rk89, Courteney Kawalek, James Martin, seth forever, Why So Serious, Jared Kennington, andrew bash, David Pillado, Jennie Hrobak, Mariana, Robbie Rogers, Michael Huckey, Anthony Torres, Luke Gilliam, David Hasenauer, Libby Higgins, Willie Lo, Mike B, and Rhagor for supporting us this week.

items.title: How does the space station never run out of oxygen? - Alvaro Romero-Calvo and Theo St Francis
items.author: TED-Ed
items.content:
<article>Explore the incredible systems that create breathable air in space, and how we can adapt that process for future expeditions.

--

For over 25 years, the International Space Station’s air supply systems have revolutionized space missions. Yet the need for regular maintenance, repairs, and upgrades has pushed engineers to develop even more reliable systems for future spacecraft. How do we create breathable air in space? And what does that look like for longer expeditions? Alvaro Romero-Calvo and Theo St Francis investigate.

Lesson by Alvaro Romero-Calvo and Theo St Francis, directed by Lazy Chief.

items.title: A Millenium of Choruses in the Cloud - An Ecoacoustics Odyssey • Paul Roe • YOW! 2025
items.author: GOTO Conferences
items.content:
<article>This presentation was recorded at YOW! 2025. #GOTOcon #YOW
https://yowcon.com

Paul Roe - Dean Research at James Cook University @jamescookuniversity 

RESOURCES
https://www.linkedin.com/in/paul-roe-24724913
https://portfolio.jcu.edu.au/Researchers/paul.roe

Links
https://www.dcceew.gov.au/environment/biodiversity/conservation/publications/australias-strategy-for-nature
https://www.un.org/sustainabledevelopment/sustainable-development-goals
https://acousticobservatory.org
https://xeno-canto.org
https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.03071

ABSTRACT
Ecoacoustics is transforming how we explore and understand our natural world by capturing the rich soundscape of our environment, from the songs of birds and the calls of frogs to the deep bellows of koalas. We’ve built the Australian Acoustic Observatory, a continent-wide network of hundreds of autonomous sound recorders, collecting the equivalent of over a thousand years of continuous audio.

In this talk, I’ll share our journey of listening to nature at scale — and how we’re harnessing the power of cloud computing and artificial intelligence to reveal the health of Australia’s ecosystems. [...]

items.title: The Secret Agent of the American Revolution
items.author: PBS
items.content:
<article>Watch the full documentary: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fMpYozsIyX0

James Lafayette is recruited to the patriot cause by the general  Marquis De Lafayette. James is a double agent, cozying up to Cornwallis, the leader of the British military, while feeding the information back to the Americans. The intelligence he gathers helps end the Revolutionary War. But after the war ends, James is sent back to slavery, and has to wait six more years for his freedom.

Declarations: Black Americans and the Revolutionary War
Follow the stories of four Black Americans as they navigate our country’s fight for independence and their individual pursuits of freedom. Discover the stories of James Lafayette, who served as a double agent for the Patriots and helped bring down the British at Yorktown; Harry Washington, who was enslaved by George Washington and escaped Mount Vernon to join the British troops; Elizabeth Freeman, previously known as Mum Bet, who sued one of the most prominent patriots in Massachusetts while living under his roof; and Abraham Peyton Skipwith, an enslaved man who used his proximity to power and his unusual literacy  to petition for his own freedom and become one of the first free Black landowners in Richmond, VA.</article>

items.title: The Deceptions That Propelled The US Into Global Supremacy In The Cold War
items.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
items.content:
<article>In the third and final film in the series, Lucy Worsley reveals the historic myths and deceptions told following the United States’ emergence as a superpower after the Second World War. We often remember the 1950s and early 1960s in America as a golden era of abundance, harmony and the American dream made real. This film reveals that to be a carefully constructed illusion. In truth, the era of America’s supremacy was a time of government deception, racial conflict and fears of nuclear annihilation.

items.title: What Was Life Like as a Soldier in the American War of Independence? | Full Series
items.author: History Hit
items.content:
<article>What was life like for a soldier during the American War of Independence? 

For a special 250th anniversary compilation series, join Luke Tomes, Louee Dessent, and Dan Snow as they explore what the battlefield experience was like for soldiers in the American Revolutionary War as the Continental Army, led by George Washington took the fight to British crown forces to take back the colonies! 

Whilst Luke is recruited into the 33rd Regiment of Foot, a proud military unit commanded by Lord Charles Cornwallis that took part in almost all major campaigns of the war, Louee enlists in George Washington’s Continental Army, joining the 6th Virginia Regiment to face the redcoats on the battlefield.

History Hit then follows Dan Snow to South Carolina to uncover the story of the 1780 Battle of Camden, where remarkable archaeological discoveries, including the remains of 14 soldiers, shed new light on the American Revolution as they are honoured in a moving battlefield memorial.

items.title: The American Civil War: Decoding Fact From Fiction
items.author: Timeline - World History Documentaries
items.content:
<article>In the second programme of this three-part series, Lucy Worsley debunks the myths behind one of the USA’s great historical landmarks: the American Civil War. At the Lincoln Memorial, in Washington DC, Lucy explains that Abraham Lincoln has gone down in history as the saviour of the union, and for ending slavery. He did it at the expense of the bloodiest conflict ever to take place on American soil, a civil war that pitted Lincoln’s ‘free’ North against the slave-owning Confederate states in the South. But Lucy reveals that Lincoln’s personal views, and the behaviour of his troops towards African Americans, were not as noble as they appeared. Then, in the South, after the war, she learns how history was rewritten in a bid to downplay the evils of slavery, and how a 1915 blockbuster film about the Civil War relaunched the Ku Klux Klan with terrifying results. Lucy visits the Georgia countryside of Scarlett O’Hara, but Gone with the Wind’s technicolor depiction of the old South and contented slaves was just part of a continued effort to whitewash history and romanticise a dark past. Back in Washington DC, Lucy meets a historian who explains that the next person to reconsider the Civil War’s legacy was Martin Luther King. Standing on the steps of the Lincoln Memorial, he demanded that the USA honour a ‘bad cheque’ African Americans had been written when freedom was promised at the end of the war. Finally, she travels to Charlottesville, Virginia, and meets locals with differing opinions on a statue of the Confederate general Robert E Lee. The statue became a fatal flashpoint in 2017, when Confederate flags mingled with Klan costumes at a mass rally - sad proof, one historian suggests to Lucy, that the Civil War has never really ended.

items.title: America Made in Virginia: 250 Years Together 🇺🇸 | PBS
items.author: PBS
items.content:
<article>This July 4th, AMERICA MADE IN VIRGINIA: 250 YEARS TOGETHER celebrates the nation’s Semiquincentennial with a star-filled tribute set in the revolutionary capital of Colonial Williamsburg’s Historic Area. Blending live performance, music, historic interpretation, and large-scale visual spectacle to create an inspiring national event that connects the nation’s founding to the present day, the special is designed as a celebration and a unifying national moment. Produced in partnership with VA250, the two-hour event will honor the past, engage the present, and inspire a season of civic renewal — all from the place where the path to independence began. From the first melodic sounds of a violin to the thunderous pyrotechnic finale, this is a program for all Americans to see themselves reflected in our nation’s continuing story. 

Broadcast live from the heart of Colonial Williamsburg’s Historic Area, AMERICA MADE IN VIRGINIA: 250 YEARS TOGETHER premieres live, Saturday, July 4, 2026, at 8:00 p.m. ET (check local listings) on PBS, PBS.organd the PBS app.

"Thoroughly researched and cleverly presented, with stunning visuals, Great Art Explained makes you realise that familiarity with a work of art sometimes makes us indifferent to its power" - Forbes Magazine, 9 July 2020

If you are affected by any of the issues in this video please go to www.samaritans.org and if you would like to donate to a mental health charity please go to MIND at -https://tinyurl.com/2vh3zn6b

Vincent van Gogh was a largely self-taught artist who didn’t pick up a paintbrush until he was 30 years old. 

Just seven years later, he would be dead. 

It was really his last four years where he developed the style we would come to know him by, and these were also his most prolific years. Once he found his way, he was making up for lost time.

items.title: The Real Reason European Cars Can't Compete
items.author: Patrick Boyle
items.content:
<article>Taking care of your health just got easier, thanks to my sponsor Zocdoc! Start here at: https://zocdoc.com/partickboyle

Europe's automotive industry is facing a historic crisis as Volkswagen weighs unprecedented factory closures and massive job cuts. However, the root of the problem isn't just high energy costs or European bureaucracy—it's China Shock 2.0. With Chinese electric vehicle (EV) manufacturers building cars significantly faster and for thousands of euros less, traditional German automakers are rapidly losing market share both at home and abroad. This video analyzes the structural trade imbalances flooding the market with subsidized EVs, explains why laying off workers won't solve Volkswagen's €6,000 per-car cost gap, and examines whether new European Union tariffs will protect domestic manufacturing or simply trigger a costly global trade war.

items.title: The Quantum Experiment That Breaks Time
items.author: PBS Space Time
items.content:
<article>Sign Up on Patreon to get access to the Space Time Discord!
https://www.patreon.com/pbsspacetime

Modern physics is weird. In quantum mechanics we have the Schrodinger’s cat thought experiment, a cat in a quantum superposition of being simultaneously alive and dead. And in relativity the twin paradox thought experiment, a space-faring twin ages less than her stay-at-home sibling. But what if we could combine these and send our quantum twin around the galaxy. Could we be simultaneously old and young? What would that tell us about the quantum nature of time? And would we need schrodinger birthday candles?

items.title: How Mudskippers Feed Millions of Migrating Birds
items.author: PBS
items.content:
<article>Watch more from this series: https://to.pbs.org/4vQBvHM
Hundreds of species and millions of individuals working together in this food web is what allows the huge flocks to feed and prepare for their long journey home. The season of Koolawa is coming to an end and as the air cools, the birds prepare for an unimaginable journey. Somehow, they find their way over featureless oceans, through storms, and mountain ranges back home.

#australia #birds #wildlife   

The Kimberley: Australia’s Wild West
This three-part series captures never-before-seen footage of The Kimberley, Australia’s remote northwest region, from a First Nations perspective, chronicling a year of extreme tropical seasons to reveal the stories hidden within the rugged landscape. 

The Kimberley spans over 400,000 square kilometers of tropical wilderness, including turquoise coastlines, towering gorges, sandy deserts and sprawling savanna. The harsh, inaccessible terrain hides remarkable biodiversity, from the fearsome crocodiles who rule the rivers, to the marsupial gliders who soar over the savanna, to the hundreds of thousands of migratory shorebirds who travel from all over the planet to feed on the rich coastline. This near-pristine corner of the world is vital refuge for countless creatures. It is also the frontline of biodiversity conservation, where Traditional Owners and ecologists are working together to save the species who call the Kimberley home. 

Watch more from this series: https://to.pbs.org/4vQBvHM</article>

items.title: Hubble Spotted Something Huge Hurtling Through Space
items.author: Astrum
items.content:
<article>Astronomers spotted a runaway supermassive black hole. 
Try Brilliant’s new interactive tutor for free and get 20% off an annual Premium subscription at https://brilliant.org/astrum/

▀▀▀▀▀▀

For the first time ever, scientists have spotted a runaway supermassive black hole hurtling through space… alone. Leaving a trail of newborn stars in its wake, this cosmic giant is defying everything we know about how black holes behave. In this video, we’re diving into the mysteries scientists are racing to solve: How is this black hole creating new stars? Why is it not inside a galaxy? And how did it escape?
